title: The Tribunal of the Heart
subtitle: Ludolph on true penance: to weep for what is done, and do no more the things to be wept for
gospelRef: Luke 11:32 (the men of Nineveh); cf. Proverbs 18:3, Psalm 130:1

stillness: Be still, wretched sinner, before you are stilled.
Let the noise of your excuses fall silent.
Here, now, while you are whole and can still weep.
gospel: When the wicked man comes into the depth of sins, he despises.
But do not despise; do not despair.
Cry out of the depths, even to the Lord.
From such a depth the men of Nineveh cried out,
Easier is the prophet's threat undone
than the humbling of penance unanswered.
behold: Let a man rise up against himself.
Let him ascend the tribunal of his own mind,
and set himself before his own face.
And when he is so placed, in the judgment-seat of the heart,
let the accusing thought stand near,
Then let a certain blood of the confessing soul
And at the last, from the mind itself,
that the man judge himself unworthy.
Judge yourself, then, in these things —
while you still can, turn your will and your ways toward the better,
lest, when you no longer can, you be judged by the Lord.
consider: True penance is, without any pause of time, to grieve for past evils,
and so to bewail what is done that you do no more the things to be bewailed.
He is a mocker, and no true penitent, who still does the thing he repents of.
If then you would be a true penitent — cease from sin, and sin no more.
For empty is the penance that a following sin defiles.
Tears profit nothing if the sins are taken up again.
You repent, you bend the knee, and you laugh —
you mock the patience of God.
If it grieves you to have sinned, do not do it; if you still do it, you are no penitent.
Despise not these little sins you call light.
No sin is so small that it does not grow when neglected.
Many drops fill the river; many grains make the heap.
Knowingly to commit the least sin is graver than the whole world.
Yet for the great sins do not despair —
for no sin is mortal while it displeases you, none venial while it pleases.
Do penance while you are well, in the very time when you could also sin.
For if you wait to repent when you can sin no more —
the sins have left you; you have not left them.
prayer: Lord, the patience of your mercy has led me to repentance:
let me not waste it by delay.
You promised pardon to the one who repents,
but to the one who puts it off you did not promise tomorrow.
So I will not despise, I will not despair —
out of the depth of my sins I cry to you.
Receive the blood of a confessing soul, poured out in tears.
Judge me now within myself, that I be not judged by you;
turn my will and my ways toward the better while I still can.
I let go what is uncertain, and I hold fast to you, who are certain.
Grant me to do true penance while I am whole,
and to be found running toward you on my last day.

latinJewel.latin: Vera siquidem pænitentia est, sine intermissione temporis, de præteritis malis dolere, et sic plangere commissa, ut non committat plangenda.
latinJewel.english: For true penance is, without any pause of time, to grieve for past evils, and so to bewail what is done that one does not do the things to be bewailed.
